metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at least one concrete merge-gate enforcement mechanism (e.g. a `rebar fsck --enforce-authorship` exit-1 command suitable for a pre-receive hook or CI step) and document how an operator wires it into their deployment model as part of child 7 and the ADR.
findings.finding: The merge-gate enforcement mechanism — described as the real security control ('the local write-gate is a fast-fail UX affordance, not the control') — is not operationally specified: the plan names no concrete implementation (pre-receive hook, CI check, rebar fsck command, reconciler gate) and no operator configuration or deployment prerequisite, leaving the primary security boundary without a grounded implementation path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Enforcement opt-in per project and enforced at the MERGE gate'; AC7 — 'with it on, the merge-gate verification flags an unsigned / unknown-author event'; Scope — 'merge-gate verify'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `rebar fsck --enforce-authorship` mode that exits 1 when any event in the log fails authorship verification under the project's enforcement policy; document its use as a CI/CD gate step (e.g. in GitHub Actions, run after `git merge origin/tickets`). This makes the merge-gate concrete and deployment-model-agnostic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'era' as a concrete data structure in the identity schema (e.g. the HLC timestamp or log-entry index of a key-add/revoke event) and specify the algorithm for determining whether a signing key was valid at the era of a given event.
findings.finding: The 'era' concept (key validity scoped to merged-log position) is the decision rule for AC4's revocation test, but the data model for era — what field records it, how it is computed, where it is stored in the identity event log — is never defined. The phrase 'merged-log position (era)' appears only once in Approach with no schema reference.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Key validity is scoped to merged-log position (era)'; AC4: 'an event signed by a key revoked at an earlier era fails while one from the key's valid era passes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC7) to assert that a project with no explicit `identity.require_authenticated` config passes the merge gate and write gate even for unsigned/unauthenticated events, confirming the opt-in default is enforced by tests.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Default install unchanged; signing and enforcement are fully opt-in' has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion that observably verifies a fresh default install does NOT enforce signing — the opt-in claim is asserted in prose but not tested.
findings.location: Success Criteria (last bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7 or as a new AC: 'With `identity.require_authenticated` absent or false, unsigned events pass the merge gate and write gate without warning (default-off verified by test).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign an explicit zero-config integration regression test (e.g., in 4e9d or 3183) that asserts a project with no identity configuration produces identical behavior to the pre-feature baseline.
findings.finding: SC-5 'default install unchanged' has no child owning an integration-level regression test asserting that a project with zero identity configuration is behaviorally identical to pre-feature. 4e9d covers replay back-compat for old events and 3183 frames enforcement as opt-in, but the composite 'zero-config install = zero change' observable outcome is not explicitly verified end-to-end by any child.
findings.location: parent success criteria SC-5 / no single child
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 4e9d (or 3183) an AC: 'With no identity configuration present, existing rebar commands (list, show, create, replay) produce identical output and no errors; verified by a before/after fixture comparison.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'deliberate creation / no auto-published email' privacy intent (Approach) with the 'every mutating event carries denormalized name+email' mandate (AC2): explicitly declare whether per-event name+email propagation to origin/tickets is intentional (and document the rationale and any opt-in mechanism), or restrict denormalized email to events by identities that have explicitly opted in.
findings.finding: The plan states in the Approach that 'a contributor's email is never broadcast to the shared branch as a side effect of a normal write' (deliberate creation intent), yet AC2 mandates that 'every mutating event carries a denormalized author name+email.' These are directly contradictory, and the plan does not declare the access posture of email propagation to origin/tickets — whether this is intentional and acceptable, opt-in, or a known caveat that needs documentation.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Deliberate creation' paragraph; AC2 — 'Every mutating event carries a denormalized author name+email'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how 'merged-log position (era)' is computed deterministically across concurrent clones: define whether era is keyed on the git commit timestamp of the merge, the lexicographic position of the rotation event file in the merged tree, or an explicit monotonic sequence number stored on key-rotation events — and confirm the chosen rule produces the same era assignment on all clones after merge.
findings.finding: The 'era-scoped key validity' mechanism is ambiguous in the concurrent-clone scenario: the plan defines era as 'merged-log position' but does not specify how this position is computed deterministically when a key-rotation event merges in from a remote clone after local events were already written — since rebar's event store uses lexicographic UUID-filename order and git merge unions two concurrent branches, an event's era assignment can differ depending on merge order, potentially causing the same event to verify under one clone's key chain but fail under another's.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Trust root = the identity's in-band keys' paragraph; AC row 4 (key rotation / era-scoped validity)
findings.suggested_fix: Anchor era to the git commit SHA at which the key-rotation event first appears in the tickets branch (i.e. the commit that introduced the rotation file), not the filename sort order. Verification reads the rotation event's `introduced_at_commit` field (written at the time of key-add/rotate/revoke) and compares it to the signing event's `introduced_at_commit` — both set by the write path at commit time. This makes era deterministic and merge-order-independent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exhaustive verification-state enum (`verified` / `unverified` / `unsigned` — or revised set) with a schema location (output schema field name, type, and what each value means) co-located with AC7 or in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The 'verified / unverified / unsigned' verification-state trichotomy is used as an enum in AC7 and the Success Criteria but its value vocabulary, exhaustive cases, and schema location are never defined — an implementer cannot know whether a key-mismatch event is 'unverified' or a third value, or where this field lives in the output schema.
findings.location: Success Criteria (paragraph 2) and AC7: 'show/fsck surface verified/unverified/unsigned'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit schema snippet to AC7, e.g.: `authorship_status: 'signed_valid' | 'signed_invalid' | 'signed_unknown_key' | 'unsigned'` stored in the event's replayed state and surfaced in `show` output, with fsck using the same enum.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable merge-gate output for a flagged event in AC7 (e.g., 'exits non-zero and emits a structured error naming the offending event id and reason') so the test assertion is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC7 says the merge-gate 'flags' an unsigned/unknown-author event but does not state the observable mechanism — exit code, structured error output, fsck report entry, or a combination — making the AC not fully evaluable in-session without guessing the intended artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7: 'the merge-gate exits non-zero and prints a structured diagnostic (event id, reason: unsigned|unknown_author) to stderr; fsck records the same finding as a `WARN identity.unverified` entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'merged-log position (era)' precisely — specify whether it maps to HLC tick, git commit SHA order, or another causal marker — and justify the substitution for git-bug's Lamport-clock-based `ValidKeysAtTime` with a statement about causal safety on the shared branch.
findings.finding: The plan introduces 'merged-log position (era)' as the key-validity scoping mechanism for the G2 key-rotation chain, but this term is never defined and its relationship to the HLC/Lamport ordering model (the causal mechanism git-bug uses for ValidKeysAtTime) is unspecified. On a shared pushable branch, git commit order is not inherently causal under concurrent pushes; the plan does not state whether 'era' means HLC tick, git commit position, or something else. git-bug's validated approach uses a Lamport total order — the substitution of an undefined 'log position' without explicit justification is a novel pattern gap.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Trust root = the identity's in-band keys' paragraph; AC line 4 ('Key validity is scoped to merged-log position (era)')
findings.suggested_fix: Explicitly declare the HLC epic (P2.1) as a dependency of child 4 (TOFU + G2 key-rotation chain) and define 'era' as 'the HLC tick of the key-add/revoke event as recorded in the merged log, establishing a causal order consistent with P2.1.' Add a note mirroring the git-bug analysis in docs/remediation-implementation-plan.md lines 145–153.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the private key lifecycle for the new asymmetric identity signing key: specify storage path (e.g., gitignored `<tracker>/.identity-key` at 0600, analogous to `.signing-key`), document that it MUST NOT be committed to the tickets branch, describe the CI/shared-deployment injection path (env var?), and add this to the Secrets section of the docs + ADR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new asymmetric private signing keys for identity authorship (namespace rebar.authorship.v1) but does not declare where the private key material is stored, how it is protected from accidental commit to origin/tickets, or whether it inherits the existing gitignore/env-only discipline of REBAR_SIGNING_KEY. This is an undeclared secret lifecycle posture on a boundary-crossing surface (signed events are pushed to the shared branch; compromise of the private key undermines the non-repudiation guarantee entirely).
findings.location: Approach section — 'Trust root = the identity's in-band keys'; AC3 ('authorship kind … signs the event's DSSE-PAE bytes via the foundation's substrate'); Scope — 'authorship attestation kind on the foundation's substrate'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing REBAR_SIGNING_KEY discipline: (1) store the private key in a gitignored file (e.g., `.rebar/identity.pem`, mode 0600) auto-generated on first use; (2) add a corresponding env-var override (e.g., `REBAR_IDENTITY_KEY`) for shared/CI deployments; (3) add `.rebar/identity.pem` to the gitignore template and document it in the Secrets section; (4) add a check in `fsck` / the write-gate that warns if a private key file is tracked by git.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the minimum schema for the `rebar.authorship.v1` attestation event body (fields: signed event reference, key id, signature bytes, algorithm) and define the write-time fallback when signing fails in an enforcement-on project.
findings.finding: The authorship attestation event kind (`rebar.authorship.v1`) is referenced as a new event type/namespace in AC3 but no schema for its contents (what fields the DSSE envelope carries, how it binds to the parent event) is specified, and the fallback if the signing substrate (the dependency foundation epic) is unavailable at write-time is not defined.
findings.location: AC3: 'An authorship kind (namespace rebar.authorship.v1) signs the event's DSSE-PAE bytes via the foundation's substrate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in ddbe (or explicitly in 2f13 with a ddbe dependency note) the 'placeholder create' variant of the identity create path: which fields are optional, whether keys can be absent, and what invariants apply.
findings.finding: Child 2f13 (placeholder creation) depends on ddbe's identity create path being adapted for system-minted placeholders (no user present, no keys initially, partial data). Neither child specifies this interface contract — ddbe's create path as described requires name, email, mappings, and keys ('mints an identity carrying name, email, mappings, keys'), but a placeholder has only (provider, external_id, display_name) with no keys. The invariants ddbe may enforce could reject or corrupt placeholder mints.
findings.location: child 2f13-0648-0e41-474d AC-1 / child ddbe-d025-5f8c-4f43 AC-2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ddbe AC-2 a note: 'The create path supports a placeholder variant where keys and email may be absent (populated later via upgrade); test that a placeholder create and a subsequent upgrade produce a valid identity.' Alternatively, have 2f13 explicitly own the placeholder-create event type as distinct from the full create.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'Modify-Reporter constraint' in the plan body (e.g., under the Jira adapter or provider-neutral mapping section), specifying when reporter can be set, the relevant Jira API permission, and the fallback behavior.
findings.finding: The 'Modify-Reporter constraint' is listed as a required documentation topic in AC #9 ('Jira accountId mapping + the Modify-Reporter constraint') but is never defined or explained anywhere in the plan body, making the doc requirement self-referentially incomplete and leaving implementers without a spec to document.
findings.location: AC #9 (Docs + ADR: '…the Modify-Reporter constraint…'); AC #5 ('reporter where permitted')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief note such as: 'Jira's Modify-Reporter permission is project-scoped and off by default; the reconciler attempts to set reporter only when the mapped accountId has this permission and falls back to leaving the field unchanged otherwise. This is the Modify-Reporter constraint referenced in the ADR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency in bde5 on e165's era-scoped key-resolution API, and update bde5 AC-3 to read 'resolved from the identity entity at the event's log-era' to match the parent's era-scoping contract.
findings.finding: Child bde5 is listed at position 3 in the parent's intended decomposition, before e165 (position 4, key-chain), yet bde5's verify logic must resolve era-scoped public keys — a capability that only e165 defines. bde5 AC-3 says 'Trust root is the identity's in-band public keys (resolved from the identity entity)' without requiring era-scoped lookup, which means bde5 can be implemented using current-state keys only, making it inconsistent with e165's era-scoped contract.
findings.location: child bde5-2bdb-d658-4969 AC-3 / child e165-377b-a8e3-49c9
findings.suggested_fix: Either reorder delivery (e165 before bde5), or add to bde5: 'Verification calls the era-scoped key resolver defined in the TOFU/G2 chain child; a stub/interface is sufficient if e165 is not yet merged.' Also add to bde5 AC-3: 'Keys resolved at the event's merged-log era, not current state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `/rest/api/3/user/search` is the correct endpoint (vs `/rest/api/3/user/assignable/search` already in use), document the 'Browse users and groups' permission precondition, and specify the degradation behavior when this permission is absent.
findings.finding: AC5 names `/user/search` as the bootstrap mechanism for resolving an `accountId` from an email when no direct mapping exists ('bootstraps via `/user/search` best-effort'). This is a new Jira REST endpoint not used anywhere in the current codebase (which exclusively uses `/rest/api/3/user/assignable/search`). `/rest/api/3/user/search` requires the 'Browse users and groups' global permission in Jira Cloud, which is commonly restricted for privacy/GDPR reasons — the very rationale the plan cites for moving off email as a key. The plan neither states this permission as a precondition nor acknowledges that it may be unavailable in the same privacy-restricted environments where `accountId` discovery is needed.
findings.location: AC5 / Approach §Provider-neutral mapping
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '/user/search' with '/user/assignable/search?project=<project>' (already verified in codebase) for the bootstrap path, or call it out as a new permission requirement and add a probe/warning when the endpoint returns 403.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether ACLI/Jira REST supports setting reporter on create AND update, document the 'Modify Reporter' project permission as a precondition, and define the detection + graceful-skip behavior when the permission is absent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts outbound 'reporter' setting as a first-class feature ('outbound assignee/reporter' in Approach; 'Modify-Reporter constraint' named in ADR requirement), but Jira Cloud's 'Modify Reporter' permission is a non-default project permission that is disabled by default and blocked in most enterprise Jira Cloud configurations. No existing reconciler code sets the reporter field, making this a new, unverified integration surface. The plan does not state the required permission as a precondition, does not specify how the reconciler detects absence of the permission, and does not define the degradation path beyond 'where permitted'.
findings.location: Approach §Provider-neutral mapping / AC5 / Docs+ADR requirement (AC9)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (264f recommended) as the owner of the provider-seam interface contract (schema + resolve + find-or-create-placeholder) and have 2f13 explicitly depend on and reference that interface.
findings.finding: The provider-seam resolve interface is split in ownership between 264f (declares the interface: 'resolve(provider, external_id)->identity') and 2f13 (consumes the same seam for upgrade logic: 'mapping key is provider's stable id'). Neither child owns the canonical mapping schema, nor specifies whether the resolve interface is read-only or can trigger placeholder creation — creating an implicit shared-state and scope-overlap risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 264f AC-1: 'The provider-seam interface also exposes find_or_create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name)->identity, used by inbound sync.' Update 2f13 to reference 264f's interface definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to 264f AC-1: 'The provider-seam interface is defined as an abstract protocol/interface (not a concrete Jira class); the Jira adapter is one implementation; a second no-op or stub adapter demonstrates extensibility.'
findings.finding: Child 264f's provider-seam AC-1 ('a provider-seam interface exposes resolve(provider, external_id)->identity') can be satisfied by a Jira-only implementation. The parent explicitly states the seam 'must not preclude' GitHub Issues / Linear, but no child AC requires the interface to be extensible or tests it against a second provider stub. This is a scope-narrowing gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Require in 264f that the seam be a named abstract interface (e.g., ProviderAdapter protocol) with at least the Jira implementation wired through it, and a test that swaps in a stub adapter to confirm the seam is real.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit blocking/ordering annotation to decomposition child 3 (e.g. 'blocked until foundation epic delivers the signing substrate') so the external dependency is visible at the decomposition level, not only in Context/Scope.
findings.finding: The decomposition (Intended decomposition, children 1–8) lists child 3 ('Authorship attestation kind on the foundation's substrate') without any stated ordering constraint or blocking notation, even though the plan explicitly declares in Context and Scope that this entire epic 'depends on the asymmetric attestation foundation' and that the signing substrate is Out-of-scope here. An implementer reading only the decomposition would not know child 3 is externally blocked.
findings.location: Intended decomposition (children) — item 3; Context ('This epic depends on the asymmetric attestation foundation'); Scope ('Out: the signing substrate itself (foundation epic)').
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Intended decomposition' section, annotate item 3 with '*(blocked on: asymmetric attestation foundation epic)*' and consider noting that items 4 and 7 are similarly constrained (key-chain signing and merge-gate verification also use the substrate).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test clause to AC #4 (or a new AC) that verifies the TOFU genesis binding: e.g., 'a test asserts that a genesis key event is correctly recorded as the trust anchor and that a second genesis event for the same identity is rejected.'
findings.finding: The Approach explicitly names 'TOFU on the genesis key' as a trust-model decision with its own security rationale, but no Acceptance Criterion tests the genesis-key binding specifically. AC #4 only tests subsequent key operations (unsigned key-add refused; revoked-key era check) — it does not verify that the first-key establishment (TOFU binding) is correctly recorded, that it cannot be silently overwritten, or that a genesis event without a key is rejected.
findings.location: Approach ('Trust root = the identity's in-band keys, TOFU on the genesis key'); AC #4.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #4: 'A test asserts that the genesis (first) key event establishes the TOFU trust anchor and that a subsequent attempt to replace it without being signed by the existing key is refused.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting AC #8 (snapshot/compaction carries folded identities + signatures) into its own child ticket so the compaction durability guarantee can be shipped and verified independently of the enforcement gate logic in concerned-covetous-smew.
findings.finding: Child 7 (concerned-covetous-smew) bundles at least two independently releasable outcomes: (a) opt-in enforcement gates (write-gate + merge-gate + fsck marking + replay-never-rejects) and (b) snapshot/compaction carrying folded identities and signatures (AC #8). The snapshot concern is a storage-layer durability guarantee that can be shipped, tested, and verified independently of enforcement being enabled — it has a distinct 'reason to change' (compaction engine vs. gate policy) and its own AC.
findings.location: Intended decomposition item 7 / AC #7 and AC #8 / child ticket concerned-covetous-smew
findings.suggested_fix: Split item 7 into 7a (enforcement: write-gate + merge-gate + fsck marking + replay-never-rejects) and 7b (snapshot compaction: carry folded identities + signatures, round-trip test). 7b depends on children 3–4 but not on 7a, so it can be parallelised or sequenced separately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the duplicate-genesis rule: specify that replay/fsck treats the lexicographically-first (or commit-order-first) genesis event as canonical and marks any subsequent genesis event for the same identity as an fsck error, and add this invariant to the fsck marking scope in AC7 / child 7.
findings.finding: The TOFU genesis bootstrap is not specified for the concurrent-clone fork case: if two clones independently mint a genesis event for the same identity entity (e.g. due to a race or an adversarial replay), they will have divergent trust chains; the plan does not describe how replay/fsck detects or resolves duplicate genesis events, leaving a gap in the G2 chain's correctness.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Trust root = the identity's in-band keys, TOFU on the genesis key'; Intended decomposition child 4 (TOFU genesis + G2 signed key-rotation chain)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Annotate child 3 in the Intended Decomposition as blocked-by the asymmetric attestation foundation epic, and specify a stub/fallback approach (e.g. skip signing in child 3 and wire the real substrate once the foundation lands) so the child can be scoped independently without a hard block on the full epic.
findings.finding: The dependency on the 'asymmetric attestation foundation' epic is acknowledged but the implementation ordering constraint is not reflected in the child decomposition: child 3 (authorship attestation kind on the foundation's substrate) requires asymmetric signing infrastructure that does not yet exist, yet the decomposition lists it as child 3 with no blocking marker — a team could start child 3 before the foundation lands and build against a non-existent substrate.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Out: the signing substrate itself (foundation epic)'; Intended decomposition child 3; Context — 'This epic depends on the **asymmetric attestation foundation** for its signing substrate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the Jira `/user/search` endpoint is reliably usable for accountId bootstrap under GDPR-mode Jira Cloud privacy settings, and document the fallback chain (e.g., try /user/search by email → try /user/bulk by accountId if already stored → fall back to project-default) with explicit acknowledgment of privacy-mode limitations.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the reconciler 'bootstraps via `/user/search` best-effort' to resolve a Jira accountId from a local identity, but this is an unverified Jira Cloud API capability claim. The existing reconciler codebase has no evidence of /user/search being used or tested. Jira Cloud's /user/search endpoint has known GDPR restrictions (query by displayName or email may return empty results for privacy-restricted accounts), and the plan's own prior art cites that 'Jira Cloud GDPR accountId — email null/hidden' — making the bootstrap path's reliability unconfirmed.
findings.location: AC line 5 — '{provider, external_id} mappings key on the provider's opaque id; the reconciler resolves an outbound Jira assignee by accountId, bootstraps via /user/search best-effort'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'or similar' in AC7 with the decided config key and its location (e.g., `[identity] require_authenticated = true` in project-level `rebar.toml`), so child stories 1 and 7 share a single, stated default.
findings.finding: AC7 uses 'identity.require_authenticated or similar' — the 'or similar' is a literal placeholder that defers both the config key name AND its location (rebar.toml? per-repo config file? CLI flag?) to the executor, which could produce inconsistent implementations across child stories that reference it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: State a concrete default: `[identity]\nrequire_authenticated = false  # opt-in per project, in rebar.toml` — executor can act on this without clarification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to child story 3 (or a Scope note) the minimum foundation interface/stub contract needed (e.g., `sign(payload: bytes) -> Envelope` and `verify(envelope: Envelope, public_key: bytes) -> bool`) so the story can proceed behind a seam without waiting for the full foundation epic.
findings.finding: The plan states child story 3 (authorship attestation on the foundation's substrate) depends on the asymmetric attestation foundation epic, but neither the decomposition nor the ACs specify a stub/interface contract that lets child story 3 proceed before the foundation is merged — leaving the executor unable to start story 3 without either blocking or making an undocumented assumption about the substrate API.
findings.location: Intended decomposition — item 3; Acceptance Criteria — AC3; Context paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an expand-contract sequence or explicit rollback path for the unconditional addition of `author`/`author_id` fields to all mutating events, or justify why this is safely additive for all known consumers (e.g., all consumers use forward-compatible schema parsing).
findings.finding: The plan adds denormalized `author` name+email fields to ALL mutating events (not opt-in), which is a forward-going event-schema change affecting every existing event-log consumer; however, no expand-contract sequence or rollback/back-out path is specified — only replay back-compat for old events lacking the field.
findings.location: AC #2 (every mutating event carries denormalized author name + email); Approach section ('Attribution always')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under the decomposition step 2 (event attribution) stating: (a) existing consumers use forward-compatible deserialization (unknown fields ignored), so the addition is safe, OR (b) provide a two-phase sequence — first release with optional fields tolerated by all readers, then enable writing — plus a back-out plan if the field must be removed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit migration/graceful-degrade plan for legacy snapshots lacking identity+signature fields (e.g., treat absent fields as 'unsigned/unverified' and document this as the defined behavior on upgrade).
findings.finding: The snapshot/compaction change (AC #8: 'snapshots carry folded-event identities + signatures') alters the snapshot schema; existing compacted snapshots on disk lack these fields, and no migration path or graceful-degrade behavior for legacy snapshots is specified.
findings.location: AC #8 (Snapshots carry folded-event identities + signatures); decomposition item 7
findings.suggested_fix: State in the spec that snapshot deserialization treats absent identity/signature fields as 'unverified' (matching the unsigned state), so legacy compacted snapshots degrade gracefully without error rather than failing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface contract the foundation substrate must expose for `rebar.authorship.v1` consumers (e.g., PAE construction entry-point, envelope type, namespace registration), and note in decomposition step 3 that this contract will be defined jointly with the foundation epic before implementation begins.
findings.finding: The interface contract between this epic's `rebar.authorship.v1` authorship attestation consumer and the asymmetric-attestation foundation substrate is unspecified — the coupling is acknowledged ('depends on the asymmetric attestation foundation') but no interface boundary, protocol, or joint-ownership note is defined. If the foundation epic ships with a different signing API surface than implicitly assumed here (e.g., different PAE construction, different envelope type, different namespace registration mechanism), child step 3 and ACs #3/#4 break with no early-warning mechanism.
findings.location: Scope section ('the authorship attestation kind on the foundation's substrate'); AC #3 ('signs the event's DSSE-PAE bytes via the foundation's substrate'); Intended decomposition step 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Interface contract with foundation' subsection under the Approach or Scope: name the specific entry-points this epic will call (e.g., `sign(payload, key) -> Envelope`, `verify(envelope, keys) -> Result`), and add a note in child step 3 that the first task is to lock that contract with the foundation epic author before writing signing code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC2's regression test to assert that existing events lacking *both* `author_id` and the new denormalized `name`/`email` fields still replay correctly (treat missing attribution fields as optional/empty, not an error).
findings.finding: AC2's regression test covers only the absence of `author_id`, but the plan also introduces denormalized `name+email` on every mutating event as an always-present field. Pre-existing events that lack both fields are not covered by the stated regression proof, leaving a gap in the end-state-observable proof for backward compatibility of the attribution change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Regression test: existing events without `author_id`, `name`, or `email` attribution fields still replay and fold correctly (fields treated as empty/optional).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit expand-contract migration plan for existing events lacking `author`/`author_id`: phase 1 adds the field as nullable (read-compat already noted), phase 2 describes a batched/resumable backfill of git event objects, and phase 3 describes when/how enforcement is activated — including idempotency guarantees so a re-run after interruption does not double-apply.
findings.finding: The plan adds `author` name+email and optional `author_id` to every mutating event but specifies no migration strategy for the large corpus of existing events already stored as git objects — there is no expand-contract phasing (add-nullable → backfill → enforce), no batching/throttling for the rewrite, and no resumability guarantee if the process is interrupted mid-flight.
findings.location: AC2 (Event attribution) and Scope section ('event attribution fields')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the schema state after rollback of the compaction/snapshot format change: either (a) specify that new snapshot fields are ignored-not-dropped by old binaries (additive-only), provide a minimum old-binary version that is safe, and assert the post-rollback state preserves all data, or (b) explicitly label this a one-way migration and document the compensating-forward procedure — attach a literal DATA LOSS RISK note if the rollback path is destructive.
findings.finding: The plan requires snapshots (compaction) to carry folded-event identities + signatures in a new format, but provides no rollback story: rolling back to a pre-feature version after compaction has run leaves the snapshot in a format unreadable by old code, and the plan does not assert what the schema state is after rollback or provide a back-out path — DATA LOSS RISK if old tooling discards an unrecognized snapshot and replays from a now-missing or incompatible event log.
findings.location: AC8 ('Snapshots (compaction) carry folded-event identities + signatures') and Success Criteria ('snapshot/compaction carrying folded identities + signatures')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the dual-write window handling: document the required sequencing (backfill must complete before enforcement can be activated, or enforcement explicitly excludes events predating a config-set epoch), and add an AC or implementation note stating that fsck/merge-gate enforcement only applies to events at or after the identity-feature activation point.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the dual-write window between when new identity-aware events begin being written and when the backfill of existing events completes: events written DURING the migration may arrive without `author_id` (old writers) or with it (new writers), and the plan does not specify how the merge-gate or fsck handles this mixed state consistently.
findings.location: AC7 (Opt-in enforcement) and AC2 (Event attribution)
findings.suggested_fix: Consider adding a `identity.enforce_after_epoch` config that scopes enforcement to events after a specified log position, preventing false-positive flagging of pre-migration events.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit blocking dependency marker on the asymmetric attestation foundation epic for child 3 (and transitively child 7), or add a spike task to nail down the foundation's signing API surface before child 3 begins implementation.
findings.finding: Child 3 ('Authorship attestation kind on the foundation's substrate') depends on the asymmetric attestation foundation epic's DSSE-PAE signing primitive, which does not yet exist in the codebase (current signing.py is HMAC-SHA256, symmetric). The plan correctly marks the foundation as 'out of scope' for this epic, but treats its API as available for implementation without a spike or feasibility gate. If the foundation's interface (key type, envelope format, namespace dispatch) changes or is delayed, child 3 becomes a rework.
findings.location: Scope §Out / Intended decomposition child 3 / AC3

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
